flashcards.front: What is the core contract of the Map interface in Java?
flashcards.back: A Map stores key-value pairs where each key is unique and maps to exactly one value. Keys cannot be duplicated, but values can be. It provides methods for insertion, deletion, and lookup based on keys.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of get() and put() operations in HashMap?
flashcards.back: Average-case O(1) time complexity. Worst-case O(n) occurs when all keys hash to the same bucket (hash collision), which is mitigated by load factor (default 0.75) and treeification threshold (8 elements).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between HashMap, TreeMap, and LinkedHashMap?
flashcards.back: HashMap: unordered, O(1) ops. TreeMap: sorted by keys (Red-Black tree), O(log n) ops, no null keys. LinkedHashMap: insertion-order (or access-order) preservation, O(1) ops, allows one null key.

Java 7: LinkedList chaining (degrades to O(n) with many collisions). Java 8+: TreeBin (Red-Black tree) when bucket exceeds 8 elements, improving worst-case to O(log n). Uses hashCode() and equals() for key comparison.
Nested interface representing a key-value pair. Methods: getKey(), getValue(), setValue(V value). Used when iterating over map entries via entrySet().

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the load factor in HashMap?
flashcards.back: Load factor determines when HashMap should resize. Default 0.75 means resize occurs when 75% capacity is filled. Higher values: less memory, more collisions. Lower values: more memory, fewer collisions.

flashcards.front: Why can't TreeMap have null keys while HashMap can?
flashcards.back: TreeMap uses Red-Black tree and implements NavigableMap. It uses compareTo() for ordering. Null would require comparison, causing NullPointerException. Since TreeMap 6, null is permitted if comparator allows it.

flashcards.front: What are the three Map collection views?
flashcards.back: keySet(): Set of all keys. values(): Collection of all values. entrySet(): Set of key-value pairs (Map.Entry). All three are backed by the original map and support removal operations.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between HashMap and Hashtable?
flashcards.back: Hashtable is synchronized (thread-safe), doesn't allow null keys/values, legacy class. HashMap is unsynchronized, allows one null key and null values, not thread-safe (use Collections.synchronizedMap() or ConcurrentHashMap).

flashcards.front: What is ConcurrentHashMap and why prefer it over Hashtable?
flashcards.back: ConcurrentHashMap provides concurrent access without locking entire map. Uses segment-based locking (Java 7) or CAS operations (Java 8+). Better throughput than synchronized Hashtable while maintaining thread safety.

If key not present, computes value using mappingFunction, stores and returns it. If key present, returns existing value. Atomic operation useful for caching: map.computeIfAbsent(key, k -> expensiveComputation())

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of merge() in Map?
flashcards.back: merge(key, value, remappingFunction) combines existing value with new value using remapping function. If key absent, inserts directly. Useful for aggregation: map.merge(word, 1, Integer::sum) counts word occurrences.
